stem: A price of ${{orig}} is increased by {{pct}}%. What is the new price?
solutionStandard: New price \(= {{orig}} \times (1 + {{pct}}/100) = {{orig * (1 + pct/100)}}\).

stem: A price is decreased from $80 to $60. What is the percent decrease?
solutionStandard: Percent decrease \(= \dfrac{80-60}{80} \times 100 = 25\%\). Note the denominator is the original value, 80.

stem: A value is increased by {{p}}% and then decreased by {{p}}%.
solutionStandard: The net multiplier is \((1+{{p}}/100)(1-{{p}}/100) = 1 - ({{p}}/100)^2 < 1\), so the final value is always less than the original. Quantity B is greater.

stem: After a 25% discount, an item costs $60. What was the original price?
solutionShortcut: Backsolve: test $80: a 25% discount is \(80 - 20 = 60\). Matches.

stem: A stock price increases by 50% and then decreases by 50%. If the original price was $80, what is the final price?
A population grows by 10% each year. After 2 years, what is the total percent increase from the original population?
solutionStandard: The net multiplier after 2 years is \(1.1 \times 1.1 = 1.21\), a 21% increase — not simply \(10\%+10\%=20\%\).

stem: Which of the following result in exactly the same final value as a single 30% increase? Select all that apply.

options.text: A 4% increase followed by a 25% increase on the new total
A 50% increase followed by a 20% decrease

solutionStandard: (A) trivially matches. (B): \(1.04 \times 1.25 = 1.3\), a 30% increase. (C): \(1.15 \times 1.15 = 1.3225\), a 32.25% increase — not 30%. (D): \(1.5 \times 0.8 = 1.2\), a 20% increase — not 30%.

stem: A shirt's price is increased by 20% to $54. It is then discounted by 10% off this new price. What is the final price?
solutionStandard: New price after increase: $54 (given). After a 10% discount: \(54 \times 0.9 = 48.6\).

stem: A price increases by 25%, and then the new price is decreased by \(x\)% to return exactly to the original price. What is \(x\)?
solutionStandard: Let the original price be 100. After a 25% increase: 125. To return to 100: \(x\% \text{ of } 125 = 25\), so \(x = 25/125 \times 100 = 20\).

stem: Item A's price increases by 40%. Item B's price increases by 20% and then by another 20% (on the new price).
solutionStandard: Item A: 40% increase, multiplier 1.4. Item B: \(1.2 \times 1.2 = 1.44\), a 44% increase. Since \(44\% > 40\%\), Quantity B is greater.

stem: A price of ${{orig}} is decreased by {{pct}}%. What is the new price?
solutionStandard: New price \(= {{orig}} \times (1 - {{pct}}/100) = {{orig * (1 - pct/100)}}\).

stem: A value increases from 50 to 60. What is the percent increase?
solutionStandard: Percent increase \(= \dfrac{60-50}{50}\times100 = 20\%\).

stem: After a 20% increase, a price is ${{newp}}. What was the original price?
A price decreases by 30% to $70. What was the original price?
A value increases by {{p}}%, then increases by another {{p}}% (on the new value).
quantityA: The actual total percent increase after both changes
solutionStandard: The actual multiplier is \((1+{{p}}/100)^2\), giving a total increase of \(2({{p}})+({{p}}/100)^2 \times 100\)%, which is always more than simply doubling the rate to \({{2*p}}\%\), since successive percent increases compound rather than add.

stem: Which of the following result in a lower final value than a single 20% decrease? Select all that apply.
solutionStandard: A 20% decrease leaves 80% of the value. (A) leaves 75% \(<80\%\), lower. (B) leaves 85%, higher. (C): \(0.9\times0.9=0.81=81\%\), higher. (D) leaves 70%, lower.

stem: A price is discounted by 20%, and then the reduced price is discounted by an additional 15%. What single percent discount, applied once to the original price, would give the same final price?
solutionStandard: Net multiplier: \(0.8 \times 0.85 = 0.68\), i.e. a single 32% discount.

stem: A price decreases by 20%, and then the new price is increased by \(x\)% to return exactly to the original price. What is \(x\)?
solutionStandard: Let the original price be 100. After a 20% decrease: 80. To return to 100: \(x\% \text{ of } 80 = 20\), so \(x = 20/80 \times 100 = 25\).

stem: A population grows by {{r}}% each year. After 2 years, the population has increased by what total percent from the original? (Round to the nearest whole percent.)
solutionStandard: The multiplier after 2 years is \((1+{{r}}/100)^2\), giving a total percent increase of \({{Math.round((Math.pow(1+r/100,2)-1)*100)}}\%\) — more than simply doubling \({{r}}\%\), since the growth compounds.

stem: One price is increased by \(p\%\) and then decreased by \(p\%\). A different price is decreased by \(p\%\) and then increased by \(p\%\) (same \(p\), possibly different starting price).
quantityA: The net percent change of the first price
quantityB: The net percent change of the second price
solutionStandard: The first price's net multiplier is \((1+p/100)(1-p/100)\), and the second's is \((1-p/100)(1+p/100)\) — the same product, just written in the opposite order. Since multiplication is commutative, the two percent changes are always identical, regardless of the (possibly different) starting prices.
